instruction: I keep fighting the borrow checker when building a tree data structure in Rust. Parent nodes need to reference children and children need to reference parents. What's the practical approach?
output:
<think>
Tree structures with parent pointers are the classic borrow checker fight. The fundamental issue: Rust's ownership model is a DAG (directed acyclic graph), but parent-child bidirectional references create cycles. You can't have `parent: &Node` and `children: Vec<Node>` because the parent would be borrowed while children are being mutated.

There are several practical approaches: (1) Arena allocation with indices — store all nodes in a Vec, reference by index. No lifetimes, no Rc, zero overhead. This is the most common pattern in production Rust. (2) Rc<RefCell<Node>> for shared ownership with interior mutability — works but runtime borrow checking and potential panics. (3) Weak<RefCell<Node>> for parent pointers to break reference cycles. (4) The `indextree` or `petgraph` crate if you don't want to roll your own.

Arena + indices is the pragmatic choice for most cases. It's cache-friendly, simple, and the borrow checker is happy because you're just dealing with integers.
</think>

```rust
use std::fmt;

// Approach 1: Arena allocation with indices (recommended)
#[derive(Debug)]
struct Arena<T> {
    nodes: Vec<Node<T>>,
}

#[derive(Debug)]
struct Node<T> {
    data: T,
    parent: Option<usize>,
    children: Vec<usize>,
}

type NodeId = usize;

impl<T: fmt::Display> Arena<T> {
    fn new() -> Self {
        Arena { nodes: Vec::new() }
    }

    fn new_node(&mut self, data: T, parent: Option<NodeId>) -> NodeId {
        let id = self.nodes.len();
        self.nodes.push(Node {
            data,
            parent,
            children: Vec::new(),
        });
        if let Some(parent_id) = parent {
            self.nodes[parent_id].children.push(id);
        }
        id
    }

    fn get(&self, id: NodeId) -> &Node<T> {
        &self.nodes[id]
    }

    fn get_mut(&mut self, id: NodeId) -> &mut Node<T> {
        &mut self.nodes[id]
    }

    /// Walk up from node to root, collecting the path
    fn ancestors(&self, mut id: NodeId) -> Vec<NodeId> {
        let mut path = vec![id];
        while let Some(parent) = self.nodes[id].parent {
            path.push(parent);
            id = parent;
        }
        path
    }

    /// Depth-first traversal
    fn dfs(&self, root: NodeId, visit: &mut impl FnMut(NodeId, &T)) {
        visit(root, &self.nodes[root].data);
        // Clone children vec to avoid borrow conflict
        let children = self.nodes[root].children.clone();
        for child in children {
            self.dfs(child, visit);
        }
    }
}

fn main() {
    let mut arena = Arena::new();
    let root = arena.new_node("root", None);
    let child1 = arena.new_node("child1", Some(root));
    let child2 = arena.new_node("child2", Some(root));
    let grandchild = arena.new_node("grandchild", Some(child1));

    // No borrow checker issues — it's just Vec<T> + usize indices
    arena.get_mut(grandchild).data = "modified_grandchild";

    // Walk ancestors: grandchild -> child1 -> root
    let path = arena.ancestors(grandchild);
    assert_eq!(path, vec![grandchild, child1, root]);

    arena.dfs(root, &mut |id, data| {
        println!("Node {}: {}", id, data);
    });
}
```

instruction: When should I use Cow<str> vs String vs &str in Rust function signatures? I understand the theory but in practice I keep making the wrong choice.
output:
<think>
This is one of the most common practical Rust questions. The decision tree is:

1. `&str` — You only need to read the string. This is the default for function parameters. It accepts both `&String` and `&str` through deref coercion, so callers are happy either way.

2. `String` — You need to own the string (store it in a struct, move it to another thread, modify it). Take `String` when you'll always need ownership. For function params, consider `impl Into<String>` to accept both.

3. `Cow<'_, str>` — You MIGHT need to own it. Classic use case: functions that sometimes return the input unchanged and sometimes return a modified copy. Without Cow you'd always clone, wasting allocations in the common case.

The practical mistake I see most often: taking `String` parameters when `&str` suffices, forcing unnecessary clones at every call site. Second most common: not using `Cow` in parse/transform functions where most inputs pass through unchanged.

For struct fields: usually `String` (owned). Use `Cow<'a, str>` in structs only when the struct has a clear lifetime and you want to avoid cloning in hot paths.
</think>

```rust
use std::borrow::Cow;

/// RULE 1: Read-only access → &str
/// Accepts &str, &String, and &Cow<str> via deref coercion
fn count_words(text: &str) -> usize {
    text.split_whitespace().count()
}

/// RULE 2: Need ownership → String (or impl Into<String>)
struct User {
    name: String, // owned — struct outlives any borrow
}

impl User {
    // Accept both &str and String without forcing callers to clone
    fn new(name: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
        User { name: name.into() }
    }
}

/// RULE 3: MIGHT need ownership → Cow<str>
/// This function returns input unchanged 90% of the time.
/// Without Cow, you'd allocate a new String every call.
fn normalize_path(path: &str) -> Cow<'_, str> {
    if path.contains('\\') {
        // Only allocate when we actually need to modify
        Cow::Owned(path.replace('\\', "/"))
    } else {
        // Zero-cost: just borrows the input
        Cow::Borrowed(path)
    }
}

/// Real-world Cow: sanitize HTML entities, but most strings are clean
fn escape_html(input: &str) -> Cow<'_, str> {
    if input.contains(['<', '>', '&', '"', '\'']) {
        let mut output = String::with_capacity(input.len());
        for ch in input.chars() {
            match ch {
                '<' => output.push_str("<"),
                '>' => output.push_str(">"),
                '&' => output.push_str("&"),
                '"' => output.push_str("""),
                '\'' => output.push_str("'"),
                _ => output.push(ch),
            }
        }
        Cow::Owned(output)
    } else {
        Cow::Borrowed(input) // no allocation needed — vast majority of calls
    }
}

fn main() {
    // &str works with both
    let s = String::from("hello world");
    println!("{}", count_words(&s));    // &String coerces to &str
    println!("{}", count_words("hi")); // &str directly

    // Cow avoids allocation in the common case
    let clean = normalize_path("usr/local/bin"); // Cow::Borrowed, zero alloc
    let fixed = normalize_path("usr\\local\\bin"); // Cow::Owned, one alloc

    // Both Cow variants work identically as &str
    println!("clean: {}, fixed: {}", clean, fixed);

    // User::new accepts both
    let _u1 = User::new("Alice");           // &str → String via Into
    let _u2 = User::new(String::from("Bob")); // String → String (no extra alloc)
}
```

instruction: How do I properly use arena allocation patterns in Rust for a performance-critical parser that creates thousands of short-lived AST nodes?
output:
<think>
Arena allocation is critical for parsers. The problem: creating thousands of small heap allocations (Box<Node>) is slow due to allocator overhead, and the nodes all have the same lifetime (they live until parsing is complete). An arena pre-allocates a chunk of memory and bump-allocates into it, then frees everything at once.

In Rust, the `bumpalo` crate is the standard arena allocator. It gives you `&'bump T` references that are valid for the arena's lifetime. This is faster than individual allocations and eliminates the need for Rc/Arc. The key insight: all AST nodes borrow from the arena, so they share a single lifetime parameter `'bump`.

The alternative is `typed-arena` (from the Rust compiler itself) which is typed but less flexible. For parsers, bumpalo is usually better because you need different node types.

For strings in the AST, use `bumpalo::collections::String` or `&'bump str` allocated from the arena — avoids thousands of small String allocations.
</think>

```rust
use bumpalo::Bump;
use std::fmt;

// AST nodes borrow from the arena — single lifetime, zero individual frees
#[derive(Debug)]
enum Expr<'bump> {
    Number(f64),
    Ident(&'bump str),  // string lives in arena
    BinOp {
        op: Op,
        left: &'bump Expr<'bump>,   // child nodes live in arena
        right: &'bump Expr<'bump>,
    },
    Call {
        name: &'bump str,
        args: &'bump [&'bump Expr<'bump>],  // slice of refs, all in arena
    },
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
enum Op { Add, Sub, Mul, Div }

struct Parser<'bump> {
    arena: &'bump Bump,
    source: &'bump str,
    pos: usize,
}

impl<'bump> Parser<'bump> {
    fn new(arena: &'bump Bump, source: &str) -> Self {
        // Copy source into arena so it shares the lifetime
        let src = arena.alloc_str(source);
        Parser { arena, source: src, pos: 0 }
    }

    fn alloc_expr(&self, expr: Expr<'bump>) -> &'bump Expr<'bump> {
        self.arena.alloc(expr)
    }

    fn parse_number(&mut self, value: f64) -> &'bump Expr<'bump> {
        self.alloc_expr(Expr::Number(value))
    }

    fn parse_binop(
        &mut self,
        op: Op,
        left: &'bump Expr<'bump>,
        right: &'bump Expr<'bump>,
    ) -> &'bump Expr<'bump> {
        self.alloc_expr(Expr::BinOp { op, left, right })
    }

    fn parse_call(
        &mut self,
        name: &str,
        args: Vec<&'bump Expr<'bump>>,
    ) -> &'bump Expr<'bump> {
        let name = self.arena.alloc_str(name);
        let args_slice = self.arena.alloc_slice_copy(&args);
        self.alloc_expr(Expr::Call { name, args: args_slice })
    }
}

fn main() {
    let arena = Bump::new();

    // Simulate parsing: sin(x + 3.14)
    let mut parser = Parser::new(&arena, "sin(x + 3.14)");

    let x = parser.alloc_expr(Expr::Ident("x"));
    let pi = parser.parse_number(3.14);
    let sum = parser.parse_binop(Op::Add, x, pi);
    let call = parser.parse_call("sin", vec![sum]);

    println!("{:#?}", call);

    // Performance: all nodes allocated via bump pointer (< 10ns each)
    // Zero individual deallocations — arena drops everything at once
    println!("Arena used: {} bytes", arena.allocated_bytes());

    // When `arena` drops here, ALL nodes freed in one operation
}
```

instruction: I have a Rust struct that needs to store a reference to data it doesn't own, but the lifetime annotations are getting complex. When should I just clone vs fight the lifetimes?
output:
<think>
This is a pragmatism question that experienced Rust developers face constantly. The answer depends on three factors: (1) how hot is the code path, (2) how large is the data, and (3) how complex are the resulting lifetime annotations.

Clone when: the data is small (strings under ~1KB, small structs), the code isn't in a hot loop, or the lifetime annotations would infect your entire API surface. A clone is O(n) on the data size but O(1) on developer time and API complexity.

Fight lifetimes when: the data is large (megabytes), you're in a hot path (millions of calls), or the borrowing relationship is simple and local. If the lifetime only adds one `'a` parameter to one struct, that's fine.

The danger zone: when a lifetime parameter on one struct propagates to 5+ other structs and functions. This is "lifetime infection" and it's a sign you should restructure (use arena, Rc, or just clone).

Arc<str> is the middle ground — shared ownership, one allocation, no lifetime annotations, thread-safe.
</think>

```rust
use std::sync::Arc;

// ❌ BAD: Lifetime infection — 'a propagates everywhere
struct Config<'a> {
    db_url: &'a str,
    api_key: &'a str,
}

struct AppState<'a> {
    config: Config<'a>,      // 'a infects AppState
    // handler: Handler<'a>,  // and everything that holds AppState...
}

// ✅ GOOD: Just clone small config strings
struct ConfigOwned {
    db_url: String,
    api_key: String,
}

struct AppStateOwned {
    config: ConfigOwned,  // clean, no lifetimes
}

// ✅ GOOD: Arc<str> for shared immutable strings (zero-copy sharing)
struct ConfigShared {
    db_url: Arc<str>,
    api_key: Arc<str>,
}

impl ConfigShared {
    fn new(db_url: &str, api_key: &str) -> Self {
        ConfigShared {
            db_url: Arc::from(db_url),
            api_key: Arc::from(api_key),
        }
    }
}

// ✅ GOOD: Lifetimes are fine when they're LOCAL and simple
struct Parser<'input> {
    source: &'input str,  // one lifetime, used only inside parse()
    pos: usize,
}

impl<'input> Parser<'input> {
    fn new(source: &'input str) -> Self {
        Parser { source, pos: 0 }
    }

    fn next_word(&mut self) -> Option<&'input str> {
        let remaining = &self.source[self.pos..];
        let trimmed = remaining.trim_start();
        self.pos = self.source.len() - trimmed.len();
        let end = trimmed.find(char::is_whitespace).unwrap_or(trimmed.len());
        if end == 0 { return None; }
        let word = &trimmed[..end];
        self.pos += end;
        Some(word)
    }
}

// Decision framework
fn decision_guide() {
    // Small data, not hot → Clone
    let config = ConfigOwned {
        db_url: "postgres://localhost/db".to_string(),
        api_key: "secret".to_string(),
    };

    // Shared across threads, immutable → Arc<str>
    let shared = ConfigShared::new("postgres://localhost/db", "secret");
    let shared2 = shared.db_url.clone(); // cheap: increments refcount

    // Local, short-lived, large data → borrow with lifetime
    let big_data = "x".repeat(10_000_000);
    let mut parser = Parser::new(&big_data); // borrows 10MB, no copy
    while let Some(word) = parser.next_word() {
        // process word
    }
}
```
